Question: A 65-kg person jumps from the first floor window of a burning building and lands almost vertically on the ground with a horizontal velocity of 3 m/s and vertical velocity of $$-9 \text{m/s}$$ . Upon impact with the ground he is brought to rest in a short time. The force experienced by his feet depends on whether he keeps his knees stiff or bends them. Find the force on his feet in each case. A. First find the impulse on the person from the impact on the ground. Calculate both its magnitude and direction. B. Find the average force on the feet if the person keeps his leg stiff and straight and his center of mass drops by only 1 cm vertically and 1 cm horizontally during the impact. C. Find the average force on the feet if the person bends his legs throughout the impact so that his center of mass drops by 50 cm vertically and 5 cm horizontally during the impact. D. Compare the results of part (b) and (c), and draw conclusions about which way is better. You will need to find the time the impact lasts by making reasonable assumptions about the acceleration opposite to the motion. Although the force is not constant during the impact, working with constant average force for this problem is acceptable.
o3 Answer:
Known data  
Mass: m = 65 kg   
Initial velocity just before the feet touch the ground  

               v⃗ i = ( 3.0 m s⁻¹ ) î  + ( – 9.0 m s⁻¹ ) ĵ  
  (speed |v⃗ i| = √(3²+9²) = √90 = 9.49 m s⁻¹)

After the impact the person is at rest (v⃗ f = 0).

The only external forces during the very short impact are the force of the ground on the feet F⃗ G and the weight m g  (downward).  We shall work with constant average forces even though the real forces are not constant.

A.  Impulse delivered by the ground  

Impulse on the person :   J⃗ = m ( v⃗ f – v⃗ i ) = – m v⃗ i

        J⃗ = – 65 kg ( 3 î – 9 ĵ ) m s⁻¹
            = ( – 195 î + 585 ĵ ) N·s

Magnitude  
        |J⃗| = √(195² + 585²) = 195 √10 ≈ 6.16 × 10² N·s

Direction  
• Upward (positive y) and backward (negative x).  
• Angle with the vertical:  θ = tan⁻¹(|Jx|/|Jy|) = tan⁻¹(1/3) ≈ 18.4° away from the vertical.

B.  Stiff-leg landing (centre of mass moves only 1 cm down and 1 cm horizontally)

Total stopping distance along the original direction  

        s₁ = √(0.010² + 0.010²) m = 0.0141 m

Assume a constant deceleration a₁ opposite to the motion:

        |a₁| = v² /(2 s₁) = (9.49)² /(2·0.0141) ≈ 3.18 × 10³ m s⁻²

Stopping time  

        t₁ = v / |a₁| = 9.49 / 3.18×10³ ≈ 2.98 × 10⁻³ s  (≈ 3 ms)

Average net force on the person  

        F⃗ avg = J⃗ / t₁
                = ( – 195 î + 585 ĵ ) / 0.00298
                = ( – 6.55×10⁴ î + 1.96×10⁵ ĵ ) N

Magnitude of the net force      |F⃗ avg| ≈ 2.07 × 10⁵ N  
Ground–on-feet force (add the weight)  

        F⃗ ground = F⃗ avg  +  m g (–ĵ)
                   = ( – 6.55×10⁴ î + 1.96×10⁵ ĵ )  –  (0 î – 637 ĵ)
                   = ( – 6.55×10⁴ î + 1.97×10⁵ ĵ ) N

        |F⃗ ground| ≈ 2.08 × 10⁵ N   (≈ 3.3 ×10² times the body weight!)

C.  Landing with bent knees  
(The centre of mass drops 50 cm vertically and 5 cm horizontally)

Stopping distance  

        s₂ = √(0.50² + 0.05²) m = 0.502 m

Constant deceleration magnitude  

        |a₂| = v² /(2 s₂) = 9.49² /(2·0.502) ≈ 8.96 × 10¹ m s⁻²

Stopping time  

        t₂ = v / |a₂| = 9.49 / 89.6 ≈ 1.06 × 10⁻¹ s  (≈ 0.11 s)

Average net force on the person  

        F⃗ avg = J⃗ / t₂
                = ( – 195 î + 585 ĵ ) / 0.106
                = ( – 1.84×10³ î + 5.52×10³ ĵ ) N

Magnitude of the net force      |F⃗ avg| ≈ 5.8 × 10³ N  

Ground–on-feet force (add weight)

        F⃗ ground = ( – 1.84×10³ î + 5.52×10³ ĵ )  –  (0 î – 637 ĵ)
                   = ( – 1.84×10³ î + 6.16×10³ ĵ ) N

        |F⃗ ground| ≈ 6.4 × 10³ N   (≈ 9.7 times the body weight)

D.  Comparison and conclusion  

• Stiff–leg landing:  ≈ 2 × 10⁵ N  (≈ 320 g)  
• Bent–leg landing:   ≈ 6 × 10³ N  (≈ 10 g)

Bending the knees increases the stopping distance and the stopping time by almost two orders of magnitude, which reduces the average force on the feet by the same factor.  The enormous force in the stiff-leg case is far above the tolerance of bones and joints and would almost certainly cause serious injury, while the bent-leg force is within what the human body can safely withstand.  

Therefore, when landing from a jump (or in any similar situation) it is far safer to bend the knees and allow the body to decelerate over the largest possible distance and time.
o4-mini Answer:
Here is one way to organize the solution.

(Throughout we take the person’s mass m=65 kg, his arrival velocity  
v⃗ = 3 î − 9 ĵ (m/s), and his final velocity after impact 0.)

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
A) Impulse from the ground  
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  

By definition the impulse on the person is  
 J⃗ = Δp⃗ = m (v⃗_final − v⃗_initial)  
Since v⃗_final=0 we get  
 J⃗ = − m v⃗ = −65 (3 î − 9 ĵ)  
 J⃗ = −195 î + 585 ĵ (Units: N·s)  

Magnitude:  
 |J⃗| = √[ (−195)² + 585² ] ≃ √[38 025 + 342 225] = √380 250 ≃ 616.7 N·s  

Direction:  
 tan θ = J_y / J_x = 585 / (−195) = −3.  
In the usual +x‐to‐+y CCW convention this lies in quadrant II, so θ ≃ 180° − 71.6° = 108.4° measured from +x.  
You can also say “the impulse points mostly upward (ĵ) and slightly backward (−î).”

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
B) Average force, “stiff legs” (1 cm drop & 1 cm slide)  
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  

We must estimate how long the deceleration lasts.  If during the impact  
the COM moves only Δy=1 cm=0.01 m upward (to absorb the −9 m/s) and Δx=1 cm=0.01 m backward (to absorb the +3 m/s), then under constant‐deceleration kinematics one finds two different decelerations in x and in y—but the shorter of the two stoppage times will dominate the net impulse.

Better (and simpler) is to note that the vertical deceleration is the more severe limiter:  
 a_y = −v_y² / (2 Δy) = −(9 m/s)² / (2·0.01 m) = −81 / 0.02 = −4050 m/s².  
Hence the vertical‐stop time is  
 t ≃ Δt_y = |v_y| / |a_y| = 9 / 4050 ≃ 0.00222 s.  

Since the total impulse magnitude is 617 N·s, an average net force (impulse ÷ time) is  
 F_net,avg ≃ 617 N·s / 0.00222 s ≃ 2.78×10^5 N.  

If you like, you can add the person’s weight mg≈65·9.8=637 N, but over ~2 ms that is negligible.  
Thus  
 Average impact force ≃ 3×10^5 N (≈300 kN)  

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
C) Average force, “bent legs” (50 cm drop & 5 cm slide)  
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  

Now Δy=0.50 m, Δx=0.05 m.  Again the vertical deceleration will give the shortest time:  
 a_y = −v_y² / (2 Δy) = −81 / (2·0.50) = −81 / 1.0 = −81 m/s²  
so  
 Δt ≃ |v_y| / |a_y| = 9 / 81 = 0.111 s.  

Hence  
 F_net,avg ≃ J / Δt = 617 N·s / 0.111 s ≃ 5560 N.  

Even if you add mg ≃ 637 N, you get F_feet ≃ 6200 N.  

So roughly  
 Average impact force ≃ 6×10^3 N (≈6 kN)  

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
D) Comparison & conclusion  
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  

• Stiff-leg stop (Δy=1 cm): F_avg ≃ 3×10^5 N  
• Bent-leg stop (Δy=50 cm): F_avg ≃   6×10^3 N  

By bending your knees you increase the stopping distance by a factor of ~50, so the average force on your feet (and hence on your skeleton) is reduced by about the same factor.  In practice that is the single most important thing you can do to reduce impact forces and avoid injury.
gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Let's break this problem down step-by-step.

We are given:
*   Mass of the person ($m$): 65 kg
*   Initial horizontal velocity ($v_{ix}$): 3 m/s
*   Initial vertical velocity ($v_{iy}$): -9 m/s (negative sign indicates downward direction)
*   Final velocity ($v_f$): 0 m/s (the person is brought to rest)

---

### A. First find the impulse on the person from the impact on the ground. Calculate both its magnitude and direction.

**Concept:** Impulse ($J$) is the change in momentum ($\Delta p$). Momentum is a vector quantity, so we'll calculate the change in both the x and y components.
The formula for impulse is:
$J = \Delta p = p_{final} - p_{initial} = m \cdot v_{final} - m \cdot v_{initial}$

**1. Calculate the Impulse Components:**

*   **Horizontal Impulse ($J_x$):**
    $J_x = m \cdot v_{fx} - m \cdot v_{ix}$
    $J_x = (65 \text{ kg} \cdot 0 \text{ m/s}) - (65 \text{ kg} \cdot 3 \text{ m/s})$
    $J_x = -195 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}$ (or N·s)

*   **Vertical Impulse ($J_y$):**
    $J_y = m \cdot v_{fy} - m \cdot v_{iy}$
    $J_y = (65 \text{ kg} \cdot 0 \text{ m/s}) - (65 \text{ kg} \cdot -9 \text{ m/s})$
    $J_y = 0 - (-585) = +585 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}$ (or N·s)

The impulse vector is $J = (-195 \hat{i} + 585 \hat{j}) \text{ N·s}$. The negative x-component shows the ground pushed him backward, and the positive y-component shows the ground pushed him upward, both of which make physical sense to stop his motion.

**2. Calculate the Magnitude of the Impulse:**

We use the Pythagorean theorem:
$|J| = \sqrt{J_x^2 + J_y^2}$
$|J| = \sqrt{(-195)^2 + (585)^2}$
$|J| = \sqrt{38025 + 342225} = \sqrt{380250}$
$|J| \approx 616.6 \text{ N·s}$

**3. Calculate the Direction of the Impulse:**

The direction is the angle ($\theta$) measured from the positive horizontal axis.
$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{J_y}{J_x}\right)$
$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{585}{-195}\right) = \arctan(-3)$
$\theta \approx -71.6^\circ$

Since the x-component is negative and the y-component is positive, the vector is in the second quadrant. We must add 180° to the calculator's result:
$\theta = -71.6^\circ + 180^\circ = 108.4^\circ$

**Answer for Part A:**
*   **Impulse Vector:** $J = (-195 \hat{i} + 585 \hat{j}) \text{ N·s}$
*   **Magnitude:** The magnitude of the impulse is **617 N·s**.
*   **Direction:** The direction is **108.4°** counter-clockwise from the positive horizontal (ground) direction.

---

### B. Find the average force on the feet if the person keeps his leg stiff.

In this case, the center of mass moves during deceleration:
*   Horizontal distance ($\Delta x$): 1 cm = 0.01 m
*   Vertical distance ($\Delta y$): 1 cm = 0.01 m (this is a drop, so we'll use $\Delta y = -0.01$ m in our calculations)

**Concept:** We can find the average force if we know the average acceleration. We can find the average acceleration using the kinematic equation: $v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2a\Delta x$. The force from the ground must overcome both the person's momentum and the force of gravity.

**1. Calculate Average Acceleration Components:**

*   **Horizontal Acceleration ($a_x$):**
    $a_x = \frac{v_{fx}^2 - v_{ix}^2}{2\Delta x} = \frac{0^2 - (3 \text{ m/s})^2}{2(0.01 \text{ m})} = \frac{-9}{0.02} = -450 \text{ m/s}^2$

*   **Vertical Acceleration ($a_y$):**
    $a_y = \frac{v_{fy}^2 - v_{iy}^2}{2\Delta y} = \frac{0^2 - (-9 \text{ m/s})^2}{2(-0.01 \text{ m})} = \frac{-81}{-0.02} = 4050 \text{ m/s}^2$

**2. Calculate Average Net Force Components:**

*   **Net Horizontal Force ($F_{net,x}$):**
    $F_{net,x} = m \cdot a_x = 65 \text{ kg} \cdot (-450 \text{ m/s}^2) = -29,250 \text{ N}$

*   **Net Vertical Force ($F_{net,y}$):**
    $F_{net,y} = m \cdot a_y = 65 \text{ kg} \cdot (4050 \text{ m/s}^2) = 263,250 \text{ N}$

**3. Calculate the Force from the Ground (Force on Feet):**
The force on the feet is the contact force from the ground.
*   Horizontally, this is the only force: $F_{feet,x} = F_{net,x} = -29,250 \text{ N}$.
*   Vertically, the net force is the upward force from the ground minus the downward force of gravity ($F_g = mg$):
    $F_{net,y} = F_{feet,y} - F_g$
    $F_{feet,y} = F_{net,y} + F_g = 263,250 \text{ N} + (65 \text{ kg} \cdot 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2)$
    $F_{feet,y} = 263,250 \text{ N} + 637 \text{ N} = 263,887 \text{ N}$

**4. Calculate the Total Average Force on the Feet:**
$|F_{feet}| = \sqrt{(F_{feet,x})^2 + (F_{feet,y})^2}$
$|F_{feet}| = \sqrt{(-29,250)^2 + (263,887)^2} = \sqrt{8.55 \times 10^8 + 6.96 \times 10^{10}}$
$|F_{feet}| \approx 265,500 \text{ N}$

**Answer for Part B:**
The average force on the feet with stiff legs is **2.66 x 10⁵ N**.

---

### C. Find the average force on the feet if the person bends his legs.

The process is identical to Part B, but with the new displacement values:
*   Horizontal distance ($\Delta x$): 5 cm = 0.05 m
*   Vertical distance ($\Delta y$): 50 cm = 0.50 m (again, $\Delta y = -0.50$ m)

**1. Calculate Average Acceleration Components:**

*   **Horizontal Acceleration ($a_x$):**
    $a_x = \frac{v_{fx}^2 - v_{ix}^2}{2\Delta x} = \frac{0^2 - (3 \text{ m/s})^2}{2(0.05 \text{ m})} = \frac{-9}{0.1} = -90 \text{ m/s}^2$

*   **Vertical Acceleration ($a_y$):**
    $a_y = \frac{v_{fy}^2 - v_{iy}^2}{2\Delta y} = \frac{0^2 - (-9 \text{ m/s})^2}{2(-0.50 \text{ m})} = \frac{-81}{-1.0} = 81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**2. Calculate Average Net Force Components:**

*   **Net Horizontal Force ($F_{net,x}$):**
    $F_{net,x} = m \cdot a_x = 65 \text{ kg} \cdot (-90 \text{ m/s}^2) = -5,850 \text{ N}$

*   **Net Vertical Force ($F_{net,y}$):**
    $F_{net,y} = m \cdot a_y = 65 \text{ kg} \cdot (81 \text{ m/s}^2) = 5,265 \text{ N}$

**3. Calculate the Force from the Ground (Force on Feet):**
*   $F_{feet,x} = -5,850 \text{ N}$
*   $F_{feet,y} = F_{net,y} + F_g = 5,265 \text{ N} + 637 \text{ N} = 5,902 \text{ N}$

**4. Calculate the Total Average Force on the Feet:**
$|F_{feet}| = \sqrt{(F_{feet,x})^2 + (F_{feet,y})^2}$
$|F_{feet}| = \sqrt{(-5,850)^2 + (5,902)^2} = \sqrt{3.42 \times 10^7 + 3.48 \times 10^7}$
$|F_{feet}| \approx 8,315 \text{ N}$

**Answer for Part C:**
The average force on the feet with bent legs is **8,320 N**.

---

### D. Compare the results of part (b) and (c), and draw conclusions.

**Comparison:**
*   Force with Stiff Legs (Part B): **265,500 N**
*   Force with Bent Legs (Part C): **8,315 N**

The force experienced with stiff legs is over **30 times greater** than the force experienced with bent legs (265,500 / 8,315 ≈ 32).

**Conclusion:**

Bending the legs is a dramatically safer way to land. The reason lies in the relationship between impulse, force, and time ($J = F_{avg} \cdot \Delta t$).

1.  **Constant Impulse:** In both scenarios, the person's momentum must change from its initial value to zero. Therefore, the total impulse ($J$) delivered by the ground is the same in both cases (as calculated in Part A).

2.  **Increased Time and Distance:** By bending his knees, the person increases the distance over which the deceleration occurs (from 1 cm to 50 cm vertically). A greater stopping distance means the impact takes place over a longer period of time.

3.  **Reduced Force:** Since the impulse ($J$) is constant, if the impact time ($\Delta t$) is increased, the average force ($F_{avg}$) must decrease proportionally.

The force of 265,500 N is immense and would almost certainly cause catastrophic, life-threatening injuries (it's equivalent to the weight of over 27,000 kg or 60,000 lbs). The force of 8,315 N, while still very large (about 13 times the person's body weight), is far more survivable and might result in fractures rather than certain death. This principle is why cars have crumple zones and why airbags are effective: they are both designed to increase the time of impact to reduce the force on the occupants.
gpt-5 Answer:
Given: m = 65 kg, vix = +3.0 m/s, viy = −9.0 m/s. After impact the person comes to rest.

A) Impulse from the ground (during impact)
Change in momentum (net impulse) is
Jnet = m(0 − vi) = −m vix i + (−m viy) j
= (−65·3) i + (−65·(−9)) j = (−195 i + 585 j) N·s.

Magnitude: |J| = √(1952 + 5852) ≈ 6.17×10^2 N·s.
Direction: up-and-left, 71.6° above the −x-axis (108.4° CCW from +x).
Note: The ground provides essentially this impulse; gravity’s impulse over the short contact time is small by comparison.

B) Stiff legs: COM drops 1 cm vertically and 1 cm horizontally (sx = 0.01 m, sy = −0.01 m)
Assume constant average acceleration opposite each component of motion.
- Horizontal: 0 = vix^2 + 2 ax sx ⇒ ax = −vix^2/(2 sx) = −9/0.02 = −450 m/s^2
- Vertical: 0 = viy^2 + 2 ay sy ⇒ ay = −viy^2/(2 sy) = +4050 m/s^2 (upward)

Average ground-force components (include weight in vertical):
Fx ≈ m ax = 65(−450) = −2.93×10^4 N (backward)
Fy ≈ m ay + mg = 65(4050) + 65(9.8) ≈ 2.64×10^5 N (upward)

Magnitude: |Fground| ≈ √[(2.93×10^4)^2 + (2.64×10^5)^2] ≈ 2.66×10^5 N ≈ 266 kN.
Estimated contact time (from |J|/|F|): t ≈ 617/2.66×10^5 ≈ 2.3 ms.

C) Bent legs: COM drops 50 cm vertically and 5 cm horizontally (sx = 0.05 m, sy = −0.50 m)
- Horizontal: ax = −vix^2/(2 sx) = −9/0.10 = −90 m/s^2
- Vertical: ay = −viy^2/(2 sy) = +81 m/s^2

Fx ≈ m ax = 65(−90) = −5.85×10^3 N (backward)
Fy ≈ m ay + mg = 65(81) + 65(9.8) ≈ 5.90×10^3 N (upward)

Magnitude: |Fground| ≈ √[(5.85×10^3)^2 + (5.90×10^3)^2] ≈ 8.31×10^3 N ≈ 8.3 kN.
Estimated contact time: t ≈ 617/8.31×10^3 ≈ 0.074 s.

D) Comparison and conclusion
- Stiff legs: ~266 kN average force (∼420 times body weight).
- Bent legs: ~8.3 kN average force (∼13 times body weight).
Bending the knees increases stopping distance and time (from milliseconds to ~0.07 s), reducing the average force by a factor of about 32. It is far safer to bend the knees on landing.

Question: Two identical objects (such as billiard balls) have a one-dimensional collision in which one is initially motionless. After the collision, the moving object is stationary and the other moves with the same speed as the other originally had. Show that both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
Answer: Conservation of momentum demands $$m_{1} v_{\text{1,i}} + m_{2} v_{\text{2,i}} = m_{1} v_{\text{1,f}} + m_{2} v_{\text{2,f}}$$ . We are given that $$m_{1} = m_{2}$$ , $$v_{\text{1,i}} = v_{\text{2,f}}$$ , and $$v_{\text{2,i}} = v_{\text{1,f}} = 0$$ . Combining these equations with the equation given by conservation of momentum gives $$v_{\text{1,i}} = v_{\text{1,i}}$$ , which is true, so conservation of momentum is satisfied. Conservation of energy demands $$\frac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{\text{1,i}}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{\text{2,i}}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{\text{1,f}}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{\text{2,f}}^{2}$$ . Again combining this equation with the conditions given above give $$v_{\text{1,i}} = v_{\text{1,i}}$$ , so conservation of energy is satisfied.
o3 Answer:
Let each object have the same mass m.

Before the collision  
• object 1 moves to the right with speed u  
• object 2 is at rest  
Therefore  

Initial momentum, p₁ = m u + m (0) = m u  
Initial kinetic energy, K₁ = ½ m u² + ½ m (0)² = ½ m u²  

After the collision the description says  
• object 1 is brought to rest (speed 0)  
• object 2 moves off with exactly the original speed u  

Hence  

Final momentum, p₂ = m (0) + m u = m u  
Final kinetic energy, K₂ = ½ m (0)² + ½ m u² = ½ m u²  

Comparison shows

p₂ = m u = p₁ (momentum conserved)  
K₂ = ½ m u² = K₁ (kinetic energy conserved)

Thus the given outcome (the “exchange of velocities” characteristic of identical masses in a perfectly elastic one-dimensional collision) satisfies both conservation of linear momentum and conservation of kinetic energy.
o4-mini Answer:
Let both balls have mass m.  Label ball 1 as the one initially moving with speed v, and ball 2 as the one initially at rest.  

1.  Check momentum  
   Initial momentum  
     p_i  = m·v  +  m·0  = m v  
   Final momentum (after the collision ball 1 is at rest, ball 2 moves at speed v)  
     p_f  = m·0  +  m·v  = m v  
   Hence p_f = p_i, so momentum is conserved.  

2.  Check kinetic energy  
   Initial kinetic energy  
     K_i  = ½ m v²  +  0  = ½ m v²  
   Final kinetic energy  
     K_f  = ½ m·0²  +  ½ m v²  = ½ m v²  
   Hence K_f = K_i, so kinetic energy is conserved.  

Thus this “swap‐of‐velocities” outcome for two equal masses is an elastic collision that conserves both momentum and kinetic energy.
deepseek-chat Answer:
Let's analyze the given collision scenario step by step to show that both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.

### Given:
- Two identical objects (let their masses be \( m \)).
- Initial conditions:
  - Object 1: moving with velocity \( v \) (let's say to the right).
  - Object 2: stationary (\( u = 0 \)).
- Final conditions:
  - Object 1: stationary (\( v' = 0 \)).
  - Object 2: moving with velocity \( v \) (to the right).

### Conservation of Momentum:
Momentum before the collision (\( p_{\text{initial}} \)) and after the collision (\( p_{\text{final}} \)) must be equal.

1. Initial momentum:
   - Object 1: \( p_1 = m \cdot v \)
   - Object 2: \( p_2 = m \cdot 0 = 0 \)
   - Total initial momentum: \( p_{\text{initial}} = m v + 0 = m v \)

2. Final momentum:
   - Object 1: \( p'_1 = m \cdot 0 = 0 \)
   - Object 2: \( p'_2 = m \cdot v \)
   - Total final momentum: \( p_{\text{final}} = 0 + m v = m v \)

Since \( p_{\text{initial}} = p_{\text{final}} = m v \), momentum is conserved.

### Conservation of Kinetic Energy:
Kinetic energy before the collision (\( K_{\text{initial}} \)) and after the collision (\( K_{\text{final}} \)) must be equal.

1. Initial kinetic energy:
   - Object 1: \( K_1 = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \)
   - Object 2: \( K_2 = \frac{1}{2} m \cdot 0^2 = 0 \)
   - Total initial kinetic energy: \( K_{\text{initial}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + 0 = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \)

2. Final kinetic energy:
   - Object 1: \( K'_1 = \frac{1}{2} m \cdot 0^2 = 0 \)
   - Object 2: \( K'_2 = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \)
   - Total final kinetic energy: \( K_{\text{final}} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \)

Since \( K_{\text{initial}} = K_{\text{final}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \), kinetic energy is conserved.

### Conclusion:
In this collision:
- Momentum is conserved: \( p_{\text{initial}} = p_{\text{final}} \).
- Kinetic energy is conserved: \( K_{\text{initial}} = K_{\text{final}} \).

This is an example of a perfectly elastic collision, where both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. The fact that the moving object comes to rest and transfers all its velocity to the initially stationary object is a characteristic of such collisions between identical masses.
gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Let's break this down step-by-step to show that both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved in this specific type of collision.

### 1. Define the System and Variables

First, we need to define the state of the system *before* and *after* the collision.

*   Let the two identical objects be Object 1 and Object 2.
*   Since they are identical, they have the same mass, which we will call **m**.
*   Let the initial velocity of Object 1 be **v**.
*   Let the initial velocity of Object 2 be **0** (since it's motionless).

The problem states what happens *after* the collision:

*   The final velocity of Object 1 is **0** (it becomes stationary).
*   The final velocity of Object 2 is **v** (it moves with the same speed the other originally had).

Let's summarize our variables:

**Before Collision:**
*   Mass of Object 1: $m_1 = m$
*   Initial velocity of Object 1: $v_{1i} = v$
*   Mass of Object 2: $m_2 = m$
*   Initial velocity of Object 2: $v_{2i} = 0$

**After Collision:**
*   Final velocity of Object 1: $v_{1f} = 0$
*   Final velocity of Object 2: $v_{2f} = v$

---

### 2. Show that Momentum is Conserved

The principle of conservation of momentum states that the total momentum before the collision must equal the total momentum after the collision.

**Formula for momentum:** $p = \text{mass} \times \text{velocity}$

**A. Calculate the total initial momentum ($P_{initial}$):**
The total initial momentum is the sum of the momentum of both objects before the collision.

$P_{initial} = (m_1 \times v_{1i}) + (m_2 \times v_{2i})$
$P_{initial} = (m \times v) + (m \times 0)$
$P_{initial} = mv$

**B. Calculate the total final momentum ($P_{final}$):**
The total final momentum is the sum of the momentum of both objects after the collision.

$P_{final} = (m_1 \times v_{1f}) + (m_2 \times v_{2f})$
$P_{final} = (m \times 0) + (m \times v)$
$P_{final} = mv$

**C. Compare:**
Since $P_{initial} = mv$ and $P_{final} = mv$, we have:
$P_{initial} = P_{final}$

**Conclusion: Momentum is conserved.**

---

### 3. Show that Kinetic Energy is Conserved

The principle of conservation of kinetic energy states that the total kinetic energy before the collision must equal the total kinetic energy after the collision. (This is the definition of a perfectly elastic collision).

**Formula for kinetic energy:** $KE = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{mass} \times \text{velocity}^2$

**A. Calculate the total initial kinetic energy ($KE_{initial}$):**
The total initial kinetic energy is the sum of the kinetic energy of both objects before the collision.

$KE_{initial} = (\frac{1}{2}m_1v_{1i}^2) + (\frac{1}{2}m_2v_{2i}^2)$
$KE_{initial} = (\frac{1}{2}mv^2) + (\frac{1}{2}m(0)^2)$
$KE_{initial} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$

**B. Calculate the total final kinetic energy ($KE_{final}$):**
The total final kinetic energy is the sum of the kinetic energy of both objects after the collision.

$KE_{final} = (\frac{1}{2}m_1v_{1f}^2) + (\frac{1}{2}m_2v_{2f}^2)$
$KE_{final} = (\frac{1}{2}m(0)^2) + (\frac{1}{2}mv^2)$
$KE_{final} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$

**C. Compare:**
Since $KE_{initial} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ and $KE_{final} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$, we have:
$KE_{initial} = KE_{final}$

**Conclusion: Kinetic energy is conserved.**

### Summary

By calculating the total momentum and total kinetic energy both before and after the collision, we have shown that:

1.  The total momentum of the system remains **mv**.
2.  The total kinetic energy of the system remains **$\frac{1}{2}mv^2$**.

Because both quantities are unchanged by the collision, we have successfully demonstrated that both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved for this specific scenario. This is a classic example of a **perfectly elastic one-dimensional collision** between two objects of equal mass.
gpt-5 Answer:
Let both objects have mass m.

Before collision:
- Velocities: u1 = v, u2 = 0
- Total momentum: p_i = m v + m·0 = m v
- Total kinetic energy: K_i = 1/2 m v^2 + 1/2 m·0^2 = 1/2 m v^2

After collision (given outcome):
- Velocities: v1' = 0, v2' = v
- Total momentum: p_f = m·0 + m v = m v
- Total kinetic energy: K_f = 1/2 m·0^2 + 1/2 m v^2 = 1/2 m v^2

Thus p_f = p_i and K_f = K_i, so both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.

Question: Find the center of mass of the structure given in the figure below. Assume a uniform thickness of 20 cm, and a uniform density of $$1 \text{g/cm}^{3} .$$
Answer: Assume origin on centerline and at floor, then $$( x_{\text{CM}} , y_{\text{CM}} ) = ( 0,86 \text{cm} )$$
o3 Answer:
Because every part of the “robot” is made of the same material (ρ = 1 g cm⁻³ = 1 kg dm⁻³) and all the rectangular pieces are 20 cm thick (into the page), the centre of mass can be obtained by treating each distinguishable solid as a separate body, finding its mass and the position of its own centre, and then using the weighted average  

          r_cm = Σ mᵢ rᵢ / Σ mᵢ .

Choose the origin at the floor, on the mid-plane of the body (so the z–axis is through the middle of the 20 cm thickness and the x–axis is left–right).  
Because the figure is left–right symmetric we know immediately

          x_cm = 0   ,     z_cm = 10 cm   (half the thickness).

Only the vertical coordinate y_cm has to be calculated.

----------------------------------------------------
1. List of pieces, their dimensions and masses
----------------------------------------------------
(All lengths in cm, masses in g)

1. Head (sphere)  
   r = 8 → V = 4/3 πr³ = 2048π/3 = 2144.7.  
   m₁ = 2144.7   y₁ = 73 + 60 + 8 = 141

2. Torso (25 × 60 × 20)  
   V = 25 × 60 × 20 = 30 000 → m₂ = 30 000  
   y₂ = 73 + 30 = 103  (mid-height)

3. Two arms (each 60 × 5 × 20)  
   V_each = 60 × 5 × 20 = 6000 → m₃ = 2 × 6000 = 12 000  
   The arms sit flush with the top of the torso, so their centres are
   y₃ = 133 – 2.5 = 130.5

4. Two legs (each 8 × 70 × 20)  
   V_each = 8 × 70 × 20 = 11 200 → m₄ = 2 × 11 200 = 22 400  
   y₄ = 3 + 35 = 38

5. Two feet (each 8 × 3 × 20)  
   V_each = 8 × 3 × 20 = 480 → m₅ = 2 × 480 = 960  
   y₅ = 1.5

----------------------------------------------------
2. Totals
----------------------------------------------------
Total mass  
   M = 2144.7 + 30 000 + 12 000 + 22 400 + 960  
     = 67 504.7 g   = 67.5 kg

Numerator for y_cm  

   Σ mᵢ yᵢ =
        2144.7·141   + 30 000·103
      + 12 000·130.5 + 22 400·38 + 960·1.5
      = 5 811 037 g·cm

----------------------------------------------------
3. Centre of mass
----------------------------------------------------
   y_cm = Σ mᵢ yᵢ / M
         = 5 811 037 / 67 504.7
         ≃ 86 cm  (0.86 m) above the floor.

   x_cm = 0   (on the mid-line)  
   z_cm = 10 cm (half the thickness).

----------------------------------------------------
Centre of mass of the whole structure
----------------------------------------------------
In centimetres measured from the floor:

          r_cm ≃ (0 , 86 , 10) cm

i.e. the mass centre is 0.86 m above the bottom of the feet, on the vertical symmetry axis, midway through the 20 cm thickness.
o4-mini Answer:
We choose a convenient origin at the “ground,” i.e. the bottom of the feet, and we choose the x–axis so that x = 0 is the vertical center‐line of the figure.  By symmetry x̄=0, so we need only find ȳ.

1)  List all of the pieces, their volumes, their masses and the y–coordinate of their centroids.  The density is ρ=1 g/cm³ and all of the “flat” pieces are 20 cm thick in the z–direction.  

   (i) Sphere of radius 8 cm  
       • Volume  V_sph = (4/3)π(8)³   
       • Mass    m_sph = ρ V_sph = (4/3)π·512 ≃ 2 144 g  
       • It sits with its bottom just at the top of the torso (see below), so its center is at  
         y_sph = (height of torso top) + 8  

   (ii) Torso 25×60 cm rectangle, 20 cm thick  
       • Volume  V_tor = 25·60·20 = 30 000 cm³  
       • Mass    m_tor = 30 000 g  
       • Let the bottom of the feet be y=0.  The feet are 3 cm tall and the legs 70 cm, so the bottom of the torso sits at 3 + 70 = 73 cm.  Hence the top of the torso is at 73 + 60 = 133 cm, and its centroid is halfway up the 60 cm, i.e.  
         y_tor = 73 + 30 = 103 cm  

   (iii) Two identical arms each 60 cm long by 5 cm “high” (in y) by 20 cm thick   
       • Volume of one arm  = 60·5·20 = 6 000 cm³,  mass = 6 000 g  
       • They sit immediately above the torso (which ends at y=133) and each is 5 cm “high,” so the centroid of one arm is at  
         y_arm = 133 + 5/2 = 135.5 cm  
       • Two of them:  m_arms = 2·6 000 = 12 000 g  

   (iv) Two identical legs each 5 cm wide (in x) by 70 cm “high” (in y) by 20 cm thick  
       • Volume of one leg = 5·70·20 = 7 000 cm³,  mass = 7 000 g  
       • They start on top of the 3 cm‐high foot plate, so their bottom is at y=3 cm; centroid is at  
         y_leg = 3 + 70/2 = 38 cm  
       • Two of them:  m_legs = 2·7 000 = 14 000 g  

   (v) One foot‐plate under the legs, 15 cm long (in x) by 3 cm “high” (in y) by 20 cm thick  
       • Volume = 15·3·20 = 900 cm³,  mass = 900 g  
       • Its centroid is at half its height above y=0, i.e.  
         y_foot = 3/2 = 1.5 cm  

2)  Compute the total mass and the first moment in y:  

   m_total = m_sph + m_tor + m_arms + m_legs + m_foot  
           ≃ 2 144 + 30 000 + 12 000 + 14 000 +   900  
           = 59 044 g  

   (We have rounded the sphere to 2 144 g.)  

   Now the moments about y=0:  
     m_sph·y_sph       = (2 144)·(133+8)  = 2 144·141 = 302 304  
     m_tor·y_tor       = 30 000·103      = 3 090 000  
     m_arms·y_arm      = 12 000·135.5    = 1 626 000  
     m_legs·y_leg      = 14 000·38       =   532 000  
     m_foot·y_foot     =    900·1.5      =     1 350  
   ————————————————————————————————  
   Σ(m_i y_i) ≃ 302 304 + 3 090 000 + 1 626 000 + 532 000 + 1 350  
            = 5 551 654 (cm·g)  

3)  Therefore the y–coordinate of the overall center of mass is  

   ȳ = [Σ(m_i y_i)] / m_total  
      ≃ 5 551 654 / 59 044  
      ≃ 94.0 cm  

and by symmetry x̄=0.  

Answer:  The center of mass lies on the vertical center‐line of the figure, approximately 94 cm above the bottom of the feet.
gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Let's find the center of mass for the given structure.

### 1. Principle and Strategy

The key principle for finding the center of mass (CM) of a complex shape is to break it down into simpler, standard shapes. We will use the **method of composite bodies**, treating the cutouts as shapes with "negative" mass or area.

Since the structure has a **uniform thickness and density**, the center of mass will be at the same location as the **centroid** of the 2D area. This simplifies the problem, as we only need to work with areas instead of volumes and masses.

Our strategy is as follows:
1.  Define a coordinate system. We'll place the origin (0, 0) at the bottom-left corner.
2.  Decompose the structure into three simple shapes:
    *   **Shape 1:** A large solid rectangle (80 cm x 100 cm).
    *   **Shape 2:** A smaller rectangular cutout (treated as negative area).
    *   **Shape 3:** A semi-circular cutout (treated as negative area).
3.  Calculate the area and the coordinates of the centroid for each individual shape.
4.  Use the formulas for the centroid of a composite body to find the overall center of mass ($X_{CM}, Y_{CM}$).

The formulas are:
$$ X_{CM} = \frac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i} \quad \text{and} \quad Y_{CM} = \frac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} $$
where $A_i$ is the area of a shape, and $(x_i, y_i)$ are the coordinates of its centroid. For cutouts, the area $A_i$ will be negative.

---

### 2. Analysis of Component Shapes

Let's set the origin (0, 0) at the bottom-left corner of the structure.

#### Shape 1: Large Rectangle (Positive Area)
*   **Dimensions:** Width = 80 cm, Height = 100 cm.
*   **Area ($A_1$):** $A_1 = 80 \times 100 = 8000 \text{ cm}^2$.
*   **Centroid ($x_1, y_1$):** The centroid is at the geometric center.
    *   $x_1 = \frac{80}{2} = 40 \text{ cm}$.
    *   $y_1 = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ cm}$.

#### Shape 2: Rectangular Cutout (Negative Area)
*   **Dimensions:**
    *   Width = $80 - 50 = 30 \text{ cm}$.
    *   Height = $100 - 60 = 40 \text{ cm}$.
*   **Area ($A_2$):** This is a cutout, so its area is negative.
    *   $A_2 = -(30 \times 40) = -1200 \text{ cm}^2$.
*   **Centroid ($x_2, y_2$):** We find the center of this smaller rectangle relative to the origin (0,0).
    *   $x_2 = 50 + \frac{30}{2} = 50 + 15 = 65 \text{ cm}$.
    *   $y_2 = 60 + \frac{40}{2} = 60 + 20 = 80 \text{ cm}$.

#### Shape 3: Semi-Circular Cutout (Negative Area)
*   **Dimensions:** The diameter is 40 cm, so the radius is $r = 20 \text{ cm}$.
*   **Area ($A_3$):** This is a cutout, so its area is negative.
    *   $A_3 = - \frac{1}{2} \pi r^2 = - \frac{1}{2} \pi (20)^2 = -200\pi \text{ cm}^2$.
*   **Centroid ($x_3, y_3$):**
    *   The center of the semi-circle's diameter is at $x=40$. So, $x_3 = 40 \text{ cm}$.
    *   The centroid of a semi-circle is located at a distance of $\frac{4r}{3\pi}$ from its straight edge (diameter). The straight edge lies on the x-axis (y=0).
    *   $y_3 = \frac{4r}{3\pi} = \frac{4(20)}{3\pi} = \frac{80}{3\pi} \text{ cm}$.

---

### 3. Calculation of the Center of Mass

Now we can compile the data and calculate the total center of mass.

| Shape                 | Area ($A_i$) (cm²)        | $x_i$ (cm)            | $y_i$ (cm)                | $A_i x_i$ (cm³)               | $A_i y_i$ (cm³)                      |
|-----------------------|---------------------------|-----------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1. Large Rectangle    | 8000                      | 40                    | 50                        | 320,000                       | 400,000                              |
| 2. Rectangular Cutout | -1200                     | 65                    | 80                        | -78,000                       | -96,000                              |
| 3. Semi-circle Cutout | $-200\pi \approx -628.32$ | 40                    | $\frac{80}{3\pi} \approx 8.49$ | $-8000\pi \approx -25132.7$   | $-200\pi \cdot \frac{80}{3\pi} = -\frac{16000}{3} \approx -5333.3$ |
| **Total (Σ)**         | $6800-200\pi \approx 6171.68$ |                       |                           | $242000-8000\pi \approx 216867.3$ | $304000-\frac{16000}{3} \approx 298666.7$ |

#### Find $X_{CM}$:
$$ X_{CM} = \frac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{242000 - 8000\pi}{6800 - 200\pi} $$
Factoring out 200 from the numerator and denominator:
$$ X_{CM} = \frac{200(1210 - 40\pi)}{200(34 - \pi)} = \frac{1210 - 40\pi}{34 - \pi} \approx \frac{1084.336}{30.858} \approx 35.139 \text{ cm} $$

#### Find $Y_{CM}$:
$$ Y_{CM} = \frac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{400000 - 96000 - \frac{16000}{3}}{6800 - 200\pi} $$
$$ Y_{CM} = \frac{304000 - \frac{16000}{3}}{6800 - 200\pi} = \frac{\frac{912000 - 16000}{3}}{6800 - 200\pi} = \frac{\frac{896000}{3}}{6800 - 200\pi} $$
$$ Y_{CM} = \frac{896000}{3(6800 - 200\pi)} \approx \frac{298666.67}{6171.68} \approx 48.393 \text{ cm} $$

---

### Final Answer

The center of mass of the structure is located at the coordinates:

$$ (X_{CM}, Y_{CM}) \approx (35.14 \text{ cm}, 48.39 \text{ cm}) $$
